document: Corruption, Drug Trafficking and Other Serious Cri (Singapore - Row 4)

sections.heading: This Act is the Corruption, Drug Trafficking and Other Serious Crimes
sections.title: Definition of foreign serious environmental offences
sections.summary: This section defines 'foreign serious environmental offence' as an offence against the national law of a foreign country involving illegal logging, land clearing, mining, hazardous waste, wildlife trade, or related activities. The definition specifies activities that contravene domestic and international laws. The definition applies to environmental offences occurring in foreign jurisdictions.
sections.dtia_summary: This section defines environmental offences that could impact data transfer assessments involving cross-border environmental data.

requirements.text: Defines 'foreign serious environmental offence' as an offence against national law of a foreign country involving illegal logging, land clearing, mining, hazardous waste, wildlife trade, or related activities

sections.source_quote: Any "foreign serious environmental offence" being an offence against the national law of a foreign country that consists of the doing of any of the following (however described):
sections.relevance_reason: Irrelevant to data compliance

sections.heading: This Act is the Corruption, Drug Trafficking and Other Serious Crimes (cont.)
sections.relevance_reason: Irrelevant: corruption law, no data handling

sections.heading: Subject to sections 10(2), 11(2) and 31(5) and (6) (whichever is applicable), a
sections.title: Confiscation order may be made despite prior order under Part 9
sections.summary: This section states that a confiscation order under section 6 or 7 may be issued against a person for an act even if a prior confiscation order under Part 9 of the Organised Crime Act 2015 has already been made against that person for the same act. The provision ensures that the court can still impose confiscation orders for the same act if the prior order was under a different part of the Act. The order applies to persons who have been charged or convicted of drug dealing or serious offences.

requirements.text: A confiscation order under section 6 or 7 may be made against a person in relation to any act despite a prior confiscation order under Part 9 of the Organised Crime Act 2015

sections.source_quote: 8. Subject to sections 10(2), 11(2) and 31(5) and (6) (whichever is applicable), a confiscation order under section 6 or 7 (as the case may be) may be made against a person in relation to any act despite the fact that a confiscation order under Part 9 of the Organised Crime Act 2015 has been made against that person in relation to the same act.
sections.relevance_reason: Irrelevant: confiscation orders for criminal acts

sections.heading: Subject to sections 10(2), 11(2) and 31(5) and (6) (whichever is applicable), a (cont.)
sections.relevance_reason: Irrelevant: corruption law, no data handling

sections.heading: Subject to sections 10(2), 11(2) and 31(5) and (6) (whichever is applicable), a (cont.)
sections.title: Restriction of realisable property by court order
sections.summary: This section allows the High Court's General Division to issue restraint orders prohibiting individuals from dealing with realisable property, with specific conditions. The orders apply to property held by the specified person, including property transferred after the order. The orders must be applied by the Public Prosecutor and require notice to affected persons. The orders may be discharged once related criminal proceedings conclude.
sections.dtia_summary: Restriction of realisable property by court order may impact data transfers if property includes data assets, requiring assessment of cross-border data movement under restraint orders.

requirements.text: General Division of High Court may make restraint order prohibiting dealing with realisable property
Restriction applies to all realisable property held by specified person
Restriction applies to property transferred to specified person after order
Restriction order must be applied by Public Prosecutor
Restriction order must provide notice to persons affected by order
Restriction order may be discharged when criminal proceedings conclude

sections.source_quote: 19.—(1) The General Division of the High Court may make a restraint order to prohibit any person from dealing with any realisable property, subject to such conditions and exceptions as may be specified in the order.
sections.relevance_reason: No data protection or cross-border transfer relevance

sections.heading: Subject to sections 10(2), 11(2) and 31(5) and (6) (whichever is applicable), a (cont.)
sections.title: Application of confiscated property proceeds
sections.summary: This section specifies how proceeds from realisable property seized under confiscation orders must be applied. After payments as directed by the court, the proceeds must be applied to satisfy the confiscation order. Any remaining funds must be distributed among those who held property realised under the Act, following court-directed proportions after providing reasonable representation opportunities.
sections.dtia_summary: Application of confiscated property proceeds may affect data transfers if property includes data assets, requiring assessment of cross-border data movement under confiscation orders.

requirements.text: Proceeds of realisable property must be applied to satisfy confiscation order
Remaining funds after payment must be distributed among property holders
Distribution must follow court-directed proportions after representation opportunities

sections.source_quote: 23.—(1) Subject to subsection (2), the following sums in the hands of the Public Trustee or any receiver under section 19 or 22 or pursuant to a charging order, that is — (a) the proceeds of the enforcement of any charge imposed under section 20; (b) the proceeds of the realisation, other than by the enforcement of such a charge, of any property under section 19 or 22; and (c) any other sums, being property held by the defendant, must, after such payments (if any) as the General Division of the High Court may direct have been made out of those sums, be applied on the defendant's behalf towards the satisfaction of the confiscation order.
sections.relevance_reason: Irrelevant: property confiscation, not data handling

sections.heading: Where a person is, by reason of section 29, to be taken to have been convicted of
sections.title: Conviction of drug dealing or serious offence: Confiscation order conditions
sections.summary: A court cannot issue a confiscation order based on a person's conviction of a drug dealing offence or serious offence unless the court is satisfied that the person has absconded and that the evidence, if unrebutted, would warrant conviction for the offence. This applies to individuals who are taken to have been convicted under section 29.
sections.dtia_summary: This section sets conditions for confiscation orders in cases involving convicted drug dealers or serious offenders, requiring proof of absconding and evidence that would warrant conviction if unrebutted. Relevant for DTIAs involving cross-border asset recovery.

requirements.text: Court must be satisfied the person has absconded
Court must be satisfied evidence would warrant conviction if unrebutted

sections.source_quote: Where a person is, by reason of section 29, to be taken to have been convicted of a drug dealing offence or a serious offence (as the case may be), a court is not to make a confiscation order in reliance on the person's conviction of the offence unless the court is satisfied — (a) on the evidence adduced before it that, on the balance of probabilities, the person has absconded; and (b) having regard to all the evidence before the court, that such evidence if unrebutted would warrant his or her conviction for the drug dealing offence or serious offence, as the case may be.
sections.relevance_reason: Irrelevant: tax, corruption, not data protection

sections.heading: [29A
sections.title: Substitute property confiscation order conditions
sections.summary: A court must be satisfied that the defendant used or intended to use property for the commission of a drug dealing offence or serious offence, and that the property is not available for forfeiture, to issue a substitute property confiscation order. The defendant is liable to pay the assessed value of the property.
sections.dtia_summary: This section establishes conditions for substitute property confiscation orders, requiring proof of property use in criminal activity and assessing the property's value. Relevant for DTIAs involving cross-border asset seizures.

requirements.text: Court must be satisfied defendant used or intended to use property for commission of offence
Instrumentality must not be available for forfeiture

sections.source_quote: Where a defendant is convicted, or is by reason of section 29 taken to be convicted, of a drug dealing offence or a serious offence, the court is to, on the application of the Public Prosecutor, make a substitute property confiscation order against the defendant, if the court is satisfied that — (a) the defendant had used or intended to use any property (called in this Part an instrumentality) for the commission of the offence; and (b) the instrumentality is not available for forfeiture as mentioned in subsection (2).
sections.relevance_reason: Irrelevant: property confiscation, not data handling

sections.heading: [29A (cont.)
sections.title: Production orders for financial institutions in drug investigations
sections.summary: Financial institutions must produce or grant access to material related to drug dealing or criminal conduct upon court order. The order requires production within 7 days, and compliance is protected from legal disclosure penalties. Financial institutions are exempt from breach of confidentiality rules when complying with such orders. The order applies to material in public bodies' possession and overrides other laws regarding material production.
sections.dtia_summary: Requires financial institutions to produce data for drug/criminal investigations, with specific timeframes and exemptions from confidentiality rules. This impacts cross-border data transfers if institutions hold foreign customer data.

requirements.text: Financial institutions must produce material to Public Prosecutor within 7 days or grant access within 7 days
Production orders override confidentiality restrictions and other laws
Financial institutions are exempt from breach of confidentiality rules when complying with orders
Compliance with orders does not constitute breach of legal disclosure restrictions

sections.source_quote: The General Division of the High Court may, if on such an application it is satisfied that the conditions mentioned in subsection (3) are fulfilled, make an order that the financial institution which appears to the General Division of the High Court to be in possession of the material to which the application relates must — (a) produce the material to the Public Prosecutor or the person duly authorised by the Public Prosecutor for the Public Prosecutor or such person to take away; or (b) give the Public Prosecutor or the person duly authorised by the Public Prosecutor access to the material, within a reasonable period, but not less than 7 days, as the order may specify.
sections.relevance_reason: Irrelevant: tax, company structure, not data protection

sections.heading: [29A (cont.)
sections.title: Financial record retention for institutions
sections.summary: Financial institutions must retain financial transaction documents for 5 years from the transaction date or account closure. Documents must be stored in a retrievable manner. Institutions must maintain a register of documents released before the retention period ends. Failure to comply results in fines up to $10,000.
sections.dtia_summary: Mandates 5-year retention of financial records, impacting data storage duration and record-keeping practices for financial institutions. Critical for DTIA as it defines data retention periods and storage requirements.

requirements.text: Financial institutions must retain financial transaction documents for 5 years from transaction date or account closure
Documents must be stored in a retrievable manner
Institutions must maintain a register of documents released before retention period ends
Failure to comply results in fines up to $10,000

sections.source_quote: A financial institution must retain, or retain a copy of, each financial transaction document for the minimum retention period applicable to the document.
sections.relevance_reason: Financial record retention for compliance

sections.heading: Where a person or an officer, employee or agent of a person, gives information
sections.title: Information disclosure by informers must not be used as evidence
sections.summary: Informers who provide information under section 45(1) must not have possessed the information at any time when it was disclosed. This section prohibits the use of informer information in civil or criminal proceedings, requires courts to conceal or obliterate entries that identify informers, and allows courts to permit disclosure under specific conditions. The informer's identity must remain protected from disclosure to witnesses or proceedings.
sections.dtia_summary: This section establishes safeguards for informer confidentiality, requiring courts to protect informer identities and prevent their information from being used as evidence. It impacts DTIAs by mandating specific protections for informer data during cross-border transfers.

requirements.text: Informers must not have possessed information at any time when disclosed under section 45(1)
Information disclosed by informers must not be admitted in evidence in civil or criminal proceedings
Witnesses must not disclose informer's name or address
Witnesses must not answer questions that would reveal informer's identity
Courts must conceal or obliterate entries that identify informers

sections.source_quote: Where a person or an officer, employee or agent of a person, gives information under subsection (1) of section 45 as soon as practicable after having the knowledge mentioned in that subsection, the person or officer, employee or agent of the person is to be taken, for the purposes of sections 50, 51, 53, 54 and 55A, not to have been in possession of that information at any time.
sections.relevance_reason: Irrelevant: tax, company structure, pure amendment mechanics

sections.heading: Where a person or an officer, employee or agent of a person, gives information (cont.)
sections.title: Assisting drug dealers to retain benefits of drug dealing
sections.summary: A person who knowingly facilitates another's retention or control of drug dealing benefits through arrangements like concealment or transfer is guilty of an offence. This includes using drug dealing benefits to secure funds or acquire property. The offence is mitigated if the person discloses suspicions to authorized officers before or shortly after the arrangement.
sections.dtia_summary: This section creates obligations for financial institutions to prevent facilitation of drug dealing benefits across borders, requiring disclosure mechanisms for suspicious activity.

requirements.text: Person must knowingly facilitate another's retention or control of drug dealing benefits
Person must not use drug dealing benefits to secure funds or acquire property
Disclosure to authorized officers before or shortly after arrangement may mitigate offence

sections.source_quote: Where a person discloses to an authorised officer a suspicion or belief that any property, funds or investments are derived from or used in connection with drug dealing or any matter on which such a suspicion or belief is based — if the person does any act in contravention of subsection (1) or (1A) and the disclosure relates to the arrangement concerned, the person shall not be guilty of an offence under this section if the disclosure is made in accordance with this paragraph, that is — it is made before the person does the act concerned, being an act done with the consent of the authorised officer; or it is made after the person does the act, but is made on the person's initiative and as soon as it is reasonable for the person to make it;
sections.relevance_reason: Irrelevant to data compliance

sections.heading: Where a person or an officer, employee or agent of a person, gives information (cont.)
sections.title: Defence for persons disclosing knowledge of criminal proceeds
sections.summary: This section outlines specific defences for individuals who disclose knowledge of criminal proceeds. To qualify for a defence, the person must prove they did not know and had no reasonable ground to believe the arrangement related to criminal proceeds; they did not know or have reasonable grounds to believe the arrangement facilitated property retention or control; they intended to disclose knowledge to an authorised officer; and they have reasonable excuse for failing to make disclosure. Additionally, for employed individuals, they must have disclosed knowledge to their employer per established procedures.
sections.dtia_summary: This section establishes defences for disclosures of criminal proceeds knowledge, relevant to DTIAs when assessing whether individuals can legally disclose information about property derived from criminal conduct without facing penalties.

requirements.text: The person must prove they did not know and had no reasonable ground to believe the arrangement related to any person's proceeds derived from criminal conduct
The person must prove they did not know and had no reasonable ground to believe the arrangement facilitated property retention or control by another person
The person must prove they intended to disclose knowledge to an authorised officer
The person must have reasonable excuse for failing to make disclosure to an authorised officer
For employed individuals, the person must have disclosed knowledge to their employer per established procedures

sections.source_quote: defence to prove — (a) that the person did not know and had no reasonable ground to believe that the arrangement related to any person's proceeds derived from criminal conduct; (b) that the person did not know and had no reasonable ground to believe that, by the arrangement, the retention or control by or on behalf of the relevant person of any property was facilitated or (as the case may be) that, by the arrangement, any property was used as mentioned in subsection (1) or (1A), as the case may be; (c) that — (i) the person intended to disclose to an authorised officer such knowledge, belief or matter as is mentioned in subsection (3) in relation to the arrangement; and (ii) there is reasonable excuse for the person's failure to make disclosure in accordance with subsection (3)(a); (d) that, in the case of a person who was in employment at the time in question and the person enters or is otherwise concerned in the arrangement in the course of his or her employment, the person disclosed the knowledge, belief or matter as is mentioned in subsection (3) to the appropriate person in accordance with the procedure established by his or her employer for the making of such disclosures.
sections.relevance_reason: Irrelevant: financial crime defences, not data protection

sections.heading: Where a person or an officer, employee or agent of a person, gives information (cont.)
sections.title: Defence for persons disclosing knowledge of criminal proceeds
sections.summary: This section outlines specific defences for individuals who disclose knowledge of criminal proceeds. To qualify for a defence, the person must prove they did not know and had no reasonable ground to believe the arrangement related to criminal proceeds; they did not know or have reasonable grounds to believe the arrangement facilitated property retention or control; they intended to disclose knowledge to an authorised officer; and they have reasonable excuse for failing to make disclosure. Additionally, for employed individuals, they must have disclosed knowledge to their employer per established procedures.
sections.dtia_summary: This section establishes defences for disclosures of criminal proceeds knowledge, relevant to DTIAs when assessing whether individuals can legally disclose information about property derived from criminal conduct without facing penalties.

requirements.text: The person must prove they did not know and had no reasonable ground to believe the arrangement related to criminal proceeds
The person must prove they did not know and had no reasonable ground to believe the arrangement facilitated property retention or control
The person must prove they intended to disclose knowledge to an authorised officer
The person must have reasonable excuse for failing to make disclosure to an authorised officer
For employed individuals, the person must have disclosed knowledge to their employer per established procedures

sections.source_quote: defence to prove — (a) that the person did not know and had no reasonable ground to believe that the arrangement related to any person's proceeds derived from criminal conduct; (b) that the person did not know and had no reasonable ground to believe that, by the arrangement, the retention or control by or on behalf of the relevant person of any property was facilitated or (as the case may be) that, by the arrangement, any property was used as mentioned in subsection (1) or (1A), as the case may be; (c) that — (i) the person intended to disclose to an authorised officer such knowledge, belief or matter as is mentioned in subsection (3) in relation to the arrangement; and (ii) there is reasonable excuse for the person's failure to make disclosure in accordance with subsection (3)(a); (d) that, in the case of a person who was in employment at the time in question and the person enters or is otherwise concerned in the arrangement in the course of his or her employment, the person disclosed the knowledge, belief or matter as is mentioned in subsection (3) to the appropriate person in accordance with the procedure established by his or her employer for the making of such disclosures.
sections.relevance_reason: Irrelevant to data compliance

sections.heading: Where a person or an officer, employee or agent of a person, gives information (cont.)
sections.title: Proving property benefits in drug or criminal offences without specific offence knowledge
sections.summary: This section establishes that prosecutors do not need to prove a person knows or has reasonable grounds to believe specific property constitutes benefits from drug dealing or criminal conduct. Instead, proving the person knows or has reasonable grounds to believe the property generally relates to such benefits suffices. This applies to multiple offences under sections 50-55A of the Act.

requirements.text: Prosecution must prove person knows or has reasonable grounds to believe property generally relates to drug dealing or criminal conduct benefits
Prosecution does not need to prove person knows or has reasonable grounds to believe specific offence benefits

sections.source_quote: it is sufficient for the prosecution to prove that the person knows or has reasonable grounds to believe that the whole or part of the property constitutes, or directly or indirectly represents, the benefits of an offence generally
sections.relevance_reason: Irrelevant to data compliance

sections.heading: The object of this Part is to impose measures for the disclosure of information
sections.title: Object of cross-border cash reporting measures
sections.summary: This section states that the purpose of Part 6A is to require disclosure of information about physical currency and bearer negotiable instruments moving into or out of Singapore to detect and prosecute drug dealing and serious offences, and to enable confiscation of contraband cash movements.

requirements.text: Part 6A aims to detect, investigate and prosecute drug dealing and serious offences through cash movement reporting
Part 6A allows confiscation of physical currency or bearer negotiable instruments moved in contravention of reporting requirements

sections.source_quote: The object of this Part is to impose measures for the disclosure of information regarding movements of physical currency and bearer negotiable instruments into and out of Singapore for the purpose of detecting, investigating and prosecuting drug dealing offences and serious offences, and to allow the confiscation of any physical currency or bearer negotiable instrument moved, attempted to be moved, or received, in contravention of the requirements under this Part.
sections.relevance_reason: Cross-border cash reporting for financial crime detection

sections.heading: In this Part, unless the context otherwise requires —
sections.title: Anti-Money Laundering and Other Matters Act 2024
sections.summary: This section provides the bill number, first reading date, second and third reading dates, and commencement date for the Anti-Money Laundering and Other Matters Act 2024. It does not contain any substantive legal obligations or prohibitions.
sections.source_quote: 66. Act 24 of 2024—Anti-Money Laundering and Other Matters Act 2024 Bill: 20/2024 First Reading: 2 July 2024 Second and Third Readings: 6 August 2024 Commencement: 14 November 2024
sections.relevance_reason: commencement/assent dates, no substantive obligations

sections.heading: In this Part, unless the context otherwise requires — (cont.)
sections.relevance_reason: Irrelevant: corruption law, no data handling

sections.heading: Any person who obstructs or hinders any authorised officer, Suspicious
sections.title: Obstruction of law enforcement officers
sections.summary: This section states that any person who obstructs or hinders authorized officers, Suspicious Transaction Reporting Officers, or immigration officers acting in the discharge of their duties under this Act is guilty of an offence. It specifies penalties for such obstruction, including fines and imprisonment.
sections.dtia_summary: Addresses obstruction of law enforcement officers, which impacts data transfer assessments by establishing conditions under which disclosures may be considered obstructive across borders.

requirements.text: Any person who obstructs or hinders authorized officers, Suspicious Transaction Reporting Officers, or immigration officers acting in the discharge of their duties is guilty of an offence

sections.source_quote: 78. Any person who obstructs or hinders any authorised officer, Suspicious Transaction Reporting Officer or immigration officer within the meaning of section 59 acting in the discharge of his or her duty under this Act or any regulations made under this Act shall be guilty of an offence and shall be liable on conviction to a fine not exceeding $2,000 or to imprisonment for a term not exceeding 6 months or to both.
sections.relevance_reason: Irrelevant: obstruction of law enforcement, not data compliance

sections.heading: Sections 50 and 53 of this Act
sections.relevance_reason: Irrelevant: corruption law, no data handling

sections.heading: Section 204
sections.relevance_reason: Irrelevant: corruption law, no data handling

sections.heading: Section 409 (as in force
sections.relevance_reason: Irrelevant: corruption law, no data handling

sections.heading: Section 476
sections.title: Counterfeiting devices or marks for document authentication
sections.summary: This section prohibits counterfeiting devices or marks used to authenticate documents or electronic records, excluding those specified in section 467. It also addresses possession of counterfeit marked materials. The prohibition applies to individuals and entities involved in document or electronic record authentication systems.

requirements.text: Counterfeiting devices or marks used for authenticating documents or electronic records
Possessing counterfeit marked material

sections.source_quote: Section 476: Counterfeiting a device or mark used for authenticating documents or electronic records other than those described in section 467, or possessing counterfeit marked material
sections.relevance_reason: Irrelevant: counterfeiting not data protection

sections.heading: Section 24
sections.title: Hazardous substances storage and use prohibitions
sections.summary: This section prohibits the storage, use, or dealing with hazardous substances in ways that threaten health or safety. The provision has been deleted by section 387 of the Act effective 01 July 2025, meaning it no longer applies. The prohibition was previously applicable to entities handling hazardous materials.

requirements.text: Storage, use or dealing with hazardous substances in a manner that threatens health

sections.source_quote: Section 24: Storage, use or dealing with hazardous substances in a manner that threatens health, etc. [Deleted by S 387/2025 wef 01/07/2025]
sections.relevance_reason: Hazardous substances storage prohibited, no data relevance

sections.heading: Section 27A(5) (as in force
sections.title: Terrorism-related financial activities prohibitions
sections.summary: This section prohibits actions that contravene specific anti-terrorism regulations, including providing funds for terrorists, dealing with terrorist property, providing resources for terrorists, and activities that assist or promote such acts. The prohibitions apply to entities subject to the Monetary Authority of Singapore's Anti-Terrorism Measures Regulations 2002.

requirements.text: Provision or collection of funds for terrorists
Dealing with property of terrorists
Provision of resources and services for the benefit of terrorists
Doing anything that causes, assists or promotes an act contrary to regulation 5, 6 or 7

sections.source_quote: regulation 5: Provision or collection of funds for terrorists; regulation 6: Dealing with property of terrorists; regulation 7: Provision of resources and services for the benefit of terrorists; regulation 8: Doing anything that causes, assists or promotes an act contrary to regulation 5, 6 or 7
sections.relevance_reason: Pure anti-terrorism financial prohibitions, no data compliance relevance

sections.heading: Section 37M(4) (previously
sections.title: Falsifying records to obtain cash payouts
sections.summary: This section prohibits falsifying records or using contrivances to obtain cash payouts or PIC bonuses. It applies to individuals and entities involved in financial transactions where false records are used to facilitate payments, particularly in the context of the Inquiries Act 2007.

requirements.text: Falsifying records or using contrivances to obtain cash payout
Assist another person to obtain cash payout or PIC bonus

sections.source_quote: Section 37M(4) (previously section 37J(4) (Cap. 134, 2014 Revised Edition)): Falsifying records or using contrivances, etc., to obtain, or assist another person to obtain, cash payout or PIC bonus (or both), etc.
sections.relevance_reason: Pure amendment mechanics, tax administration, share capital

sections.heading: Section 6
sections.title: Payments related to trafficked victims
sections.summary: This section prohibits receiving payments connected to the exploitation of trafficked victims. The prohibition applies to individuals and entities that facilitate or receive financial transactions related to human trafficking activities, as defined under the Remote Gambling Act 2014.

requirements.text: Receive payments in connection with exploitation of trafficked victims

sections.source_quote: Section 6: Persons who receive payments in connection with exploitation of trafficked victims
sections.relevance_reason: Pure amendment mechanics, tax administration irrelevant

sections.heading: Section 26(3)
sections.title: Failure to comply with organised crime prevention orders
sections.summary: This section prohibits failing to comply with organised crime prevention orders or financial reporting orders. The prohibition applies to individuals and entities subject to such orders, including those involved in financial reporting and crime prevention activities.

requirements.text: Fail to comply with an organised crime prevention order
Fail to comply with a financial reporting order

sections.source_quote: Section 26(3): Failure to comply with an organised crime prevention order or a financial reporting order
sections.relevance_reason: Pure amendment mechanics, no data protection/privacy focus

sections.heading: Section 26(5)
sections.title: False information for organised crime prevention orders
sections.summary: This section prohibits providing false or misleading information to authorities when applying for an organised crime prevention order or financial reporting order. The prohibition applies to individuals who submit such information to the relevant authorities. The section does not specify the consequences for non-compliance.

requirements.text: Must not provide false or misleading information for an organised crime prevention order
Must not provide false or misleading information for a financial reporting order

sections.source_quote: Providing false or misleading information for an organised crime prevention order or a financial reporting order
sections.relevance_reason: No data protection or cross-border transfer relevance

sections.heading: Section 9 (previously section
sections.title: Personal information obtained in contravention of certain provisions
sections.summary: This section addresses the supply of personal information that was obtained in violation of specific legal provisions. It applies to individuals who have unlawfully acquired personal information and then share it with others. The section does not specify the penalties for such actions.

requirements.text: May supply personal information obtained in contravention of certain provisions

sections.source_quote: Supplying, etc., personal information obtained in contravention of certain provisions
sections.relevance_reason: No data protection or cross-border transfer relevance

sections.heading: Section 407
sections.title: Criminal breach of trust of property for transportation or storage
sections.summary: This section criminalizes the breach of trust when property is entrusted for transportation or storage purposes. It applies to individuals who misuse property entrusted to them for these specific purposes. The section does not specify the penalties for such breaches.

requirements.text: Criminal breach of trust of property entrusted for transportation or storage

sections.source_quote: Criminal breach of trust of property entrusted for purposes of transportation or storage
sections.relevance_reason: Criminal breach of trust not relevant to data compliance

sections.heading: Section 409
sections.title: Criminal breach of trust by public servants and financial institutions
sections.summary: This section criminalizes the breach of trust by public servants, bankers, merchants, agents, directors, officers, partners, key executives, and fiduciaries. It applies to individuals who misuse entrusted property or assets. The section does not specify the penalties for such breaches.

requirements.text: Criminal breach of trust by public servant, banker, merchant, agent, director, officer, partner, key executive or fiduciary

sections.source_quote: Criminal breach of trust by public servant, or by banker, merchant, agent, director, officer, partner, key executive or fiduciary
sections.relevance_reason: Criminal breach of trust not data protection

sections.heading: Section 416A
sections.title: Illegally obtained personal information
sections.summary: This section defines illegally obtained personal information as information acquired through unlawful means. It applies to any personal information that was obtained in violation of legal provisions. The section does not specify the consequences for possessing such information.

requirements.text: Illegally obtained personal information

sections.source_quote: Illegally obtained personal information
sections.relevance_reason: Irrelevant: defines illegal info without compliance impact

sections.heading: Section 416(2)
sections.title: Obtaining money as inducement for bankruptcy discharge
sections.summary: This section prohibits obtaining or receiving money, property, or security as inducement to refrain from opposing or consenting to the discharge of bankruptcy. It applies to individuals who seek to influence bankruptcy proceedings through financial incentives. The section does not specify the penalties for such actions.

requirements.text: Must not obtain or receive any money, property or security as inducement for forbearing to oppose, or for consenting to, discharge of bankrupt

sections.source_quote: Obtaining or receiving any money, property or security as inducement for forbearing to oppose, or for consenting to, discharge of bankrupt
sections.relevance_reason: Irrelevant: financial crime law, not data protection

sections.heading: Section 39B
sections.title: Unlawful SIM card provision using personal information
sections.summary: This section prohibits the provision of SIM cards registered with a person's personal information without authorization. It targets unauthorized use of personal data for telecommunications services. The prohibition applies to entities that register or provide SIM cards using personal information without valid consent or legal basis.
sections.dtia_summary: This section addresses unauthorized use of personal data for telecommunications services, which could impact data transfer assessments involving SIM card registrations across borders.

requirements.text: Prohibits provision of SIM cards registered using a person's personal information without authorization

sections.source_quote: Section 39B: Unlawful provision of SIM card registered using person's personal information
sections.relevance_reason: No data protection or cross-border transfer relevance

sections.heading: Section 39D
sections.title: SIM card transacting for criminal activity using another's personal information
sections.summary: This section prohibits the transacting of SIM cards registered with another person's personal information for criminal activities. It targets the misuse of personal data in telecommunications services for illegal purposes. The prohibition applies to entities facilitating such transactions.
sections.dtia_summary: This section restricts the use of personal data for criminal activities, which could affect data transfer assessments involving SIM card registrations across borders.

requirements.text: Prohibits transacting SIM cards registered using another person's personal information for criminal activity

sections.source_quote: Section 39D: Transacting of SIM card registered using another person's personal information for criminal activity
sections.relevance_reason: Irrelevant to data compliance

sections.heading: Section 39E
sections.title: SIM card transacting for consideration using another's personal information
sections.summary: This section prohibits the transacting of SIM cards registered with another person's personal information for consideration. It targets the misuse of personal data in telecommunications services for commercial purposes. The prohibition applies to entities facilitating such transactions.
sections.dtia_summary: This section restricts the use of personal data for commercial transactions, which could impact data transfer assessments involving SIM card registrations across borders.

requirements.text: Prohibits transacting SIM cards registered using another person's personal information for consideration

sections.source_quote: Section 39E: Transacting of SIM card registered using another person's personal information for consideration
sections.relevance_reason: No data protection or cross-border transfer relevance

sections.heading: Section 39F
sections.title: Possession of SIM card registered using another's personal information for criminal activity
sections.summary: This section prohibits the possession of SIM cards registered with another person's personal information for criminal activities. It targets the unauthorized use of personal data in telecommunications services for illegal purposes. The prohibition applies to entities that possess such SIM cards.
sections.dtia_summary: This section addresses unauthorized possession of SIM cards for criminal activities, which could affect data transfer assessments involving personal data across borders.

requirements.text: Prohibits possession of SIM cards registered using another person's personal information for criminal activity

sections.source_quote: Section 39F: Possession of SIM card registered using another person's personal information for criminal activity
sections.relevance_reason: Prohibits unauthorized personal data use in telecom for crime

sections.heading: Section 37S(4)
sections.title: Falsifying records for cash payout in multinational enterprises
sections.summary: This section prohibits falsifying records or using contrivances to obtain or assist others in obtaining cash payouts. It targets fraudulent activities related to multinational enterprise tax compliance. The prohibition applies to entities involved in multinational enterprise tax reporting.
sections.dtia_summary: This section addresses fraudulent cash transactions in multinational enterprises, which could impact data transfer assessments involving financial data across borders.

requirements.text: Prohibits falsifying records or using contrivances to obtain cash payouts

sections.source_quote: Section 37S(4): Falsifying records or using contrivances, etc., to obtain, or assist another person to obtain, cash payout, etc.
sections.relevance_reason: Pure financial fraud, no data protection/privacy focus

sections.heading: Any "foreign serious environmental offence" being an offence against the national law of a foreign
sections.title: Definition of foreign serious environmental offences
sections.summary: This section defines 'foreign serious environmental offence' as an offence against the national law of a foreign country involving illegal logging, land clearing, mining, hazardous waste, wildlife trade, or related activities. The definition specifies activities that contravene domestic and international laws. The definition applies to environmental offences occurring in foreign jurisdictions.
sections.dtia_summary: This section defines environmental offences that could impact data transfer assessments involving cross-border environmental data.

requirements.text: Defines 'foreign serious environmental offence' as an offence against national law of a foreign country involving illegal logging, land clearing, mining, hazardous waste, wildlife trade, or related activities

sections.source_quote: Any "foreign serious environmental offence" being an offence against the national law of a foreign country that consists of the doing of any of the following (however described):
sections.relevance_reason: Irrelevant to data compliance

sections.heading: Any "foreign serious tax offence", being an offence against the national law of a foreign country
sections.title: Definition of foreign serious tax offence
sections.summary: This section defines a 'foreign serious tax offence' as a tax-related offence against a foreign country's national law that involves wilfully evading or assisting in evading tax through specific actions such as omitting information from returns, making false statements, providing false answers, failing to correct incorrect information, maintaining false records, or using fraud. These actions must be done with intent to evade tax.

requirements.text: Omitting from, or understating or overstating in a return any information which should be included
Making any false statement or entry in any return, claim or application
Giving any false answer to any question or request for information
Failing to inform the tax authority of incorrect information in an assessment
Preparing or maintaining false books of account or records
Using fraud, art or contrivance to evade tax

sections.source_quote: 2. Any "foreign serious tax offence", being an offence against the national law of a foreign country that consists of the doing of any of the following (however described) wilfully with intent to evade, or to assist any other person to evade, any tax of that country: (a) omitting from, or understating or overstating in a return made for the purposes of that tax any information which should be included in the return; (b) making any false statement or entry in any return, claim or application made, or any document or information required to be given, for the purposes of that tax; (c) giving any false answer, whether verbally or in writing, to any question or request for information asked or made for the purposes of that tax; (d) failing to inform the authority responsible for the collection of that tax, in the required manner, of any incorrect information appearing in any assessment made by that authority, when required to do so; (e) preparing or maintaining, or authorising the preparation or maintenance, of any false books of account or other records, or falsifying or authorising the falsification of any books of account or records; (f) making use of any fraud, art or contrivance, or authorising the use of any such fraud, art or contrivance.
sections.relevance_reason: Irrelevant: tax evasion, not data protection

sections.heading: Act 9 of 2018—Cybersecurity Act 2018
sections.title: Cybersecurity Act 2018 First Reading details
sections.summary: This section provides the dates of the first, second, and third readings of the Cybersecurity Act 2018 bill in the Singapore Parliament, along with the bill number and commencement date. It does not contain any substantive legal obligations or prohibitions.
sections.source_quote: 42. Act 9 of 2018—Cybersecurity Act 2018 Date of First Reading: 8 January 2018 (Bill /2018) Date of Second and Third Readings: 5 February 2018 Date of commencement: 31 August 2018
sections.relevance_reason: Only parliamentary process dates, no legal obligations

sections.heading: Act 7 of 2019—Precious Stones and Precious Metals (Prevention of Money Laundering
sections.title: Precious Stones and Precious Metals (Prevention of Money Laundering) Act 2019
sections.summary: This section provides the dates of the first, second, and third readings of the Precious Stones and Precious Metals (Prevention of Money Laundering and Terrorism Financing) Act 2019 bill in the Singapore Parliament, along with the bill number and commencement date. It does not contain any substantive legal obligations or prohibitions.
sections.source_quote: 44. Act 7 of 2019—Precious Stones and Precious Metals (Prevention of Money Laundering and Terrorism Financing) Act 2019 Date of First Reading: 14 January 2019 (Bill /2019) Date of Second and Third Readings: 11 February 2019 Date of commencement: 10 April 2019
sections.relevance_reason: Pure commencement date and parliamentary process

sections.heading: Act 24 of 2024—Anti-Money Laundering and Other Matters Act 2024
sections.title: Anti-Money Laundering and Other Matters Act 2024
sections.summary: This section provides the bill number, first reading date, second and third reading dates, and commencement date for the Anti-Money Laundering and Other Matters Act 2024. It does not contain any substantive legal obligations or prohibitions.
sections.source_quote: 66. Act 24 of 2024—Anti-Money Laundering and Other Matters Act 2024 Bill: 20/2024 First Reading: 2 July 2024 Second and Third Readings: 6 August 2024 Commencement: 14 November 2024
sections.relevance_reason: commencement/assent dates, no substantive obligations

sections.heading: G.N. No. S 256/2026—Corruption, Drug Trafficking and Other Serious Crimes
sections.title: Corruption, Drug Trafficking and Other Serious Crimes (Confiscation of Benefits) Act 1992 Amendment Order 2026
sections.summary: This section details the commencement date of the Amendment Order 2026 to the Corruption, Drug Trafficking and Other Serious Crimes (Confiscation of Benefits) Act 1992, which amends the Second Schedule of the Act. It provides a comparative table showing renumbering of sections between the 2000 and 2020 editions of the Act, with some sections deleted by subsequent legislation.
sections.source_quote: 74. G.N. No. S 256/2026—Corruption, Drug Trafficking and Other Serious Crimes (Confiscation of Benefits) Act 1992 (Amendment of Second Schedule) () Order 2026 Date of commencement: 1 May 2026
sections.relevance_reason: commencement date, renumbering, no data compliance relevance
